title_de: Die bewegende Geschichte hinter Don McLeans ikonischer Ballade 'Mountains o' Mourne'
title_nl: Ontdek de Magie van de Bergen: Het Hartverwarmende Verhaal Achter Don McLean's Iconische Ballade 'Mountains o' Mourne'
content_de:
Mountains o' Mourne – Irlands berühmtester Wanderberg und das Lied, das ihn unsterblich machte

Was ist „Mountains o' Mourne"?

Es gibt Landschaften, die sich ins Gedächtnis brennen. Die Mountains of Mourne in County Down, Nordirland, gehören dazu. Steil und dunkel steigen die Gipfel aus der Irischen See auf – eine Kulisse, die Dichter, Maler und Wanderer seit Jahrhunderten in ihren Bann zieht.

Das bekannteste Zeugnis dieser Faszination ist das Lied „Mountains of Mourne". Don McLean hat es 1971 auf seinem Album American Pie aufgenommen und damit einer irischen Volksmelodie Weltruhm beschert. Wer die Mournes einmal gesehen hat, versteht sofort, warum dieses Lied bis heute Menschen berührt.

Wer hat „Mountains O' Mourne" geschrieben?

Das Lied stammt von Percy French – Dichter, Komponist, Maler und wohl einer der charmantesten Entertainer, den Irland je hervorgebracht hat. French wurde am 1. Mai 1854 in Roscommon geboren und starb am 24. Juli 1920. Er war bekannt für seinen feinen Humor, seine Beobachtungsgabe und seine Fähigkeit, das Alltagsleben der irischen Landbevölkerung in eingängige Verse zu fassen.

Zu seinen bekanntesten Werken zählen neben „Mountains of Mourne" auch „Abdul Abulbul Amir" und „Are Ye Right, There Michael?". Der genaue Entstehungszeitpunkt von „Mountains of Mourne" ist nicht überliefert, doch Musikwissenschaftler datieren es auf das späte 19. Jahrhundert.

Wo liegen die Mountains of Mourne?

Die Mountains of Mourne liegen in County Down, Nordirland, und erstrecken sich über rund 570 Quadratkilometer. Das Massiv umfasst zwölf Gipfel über 600 Meter – darunter Slieve Donard mit 849 Metern, dem höchsten Berg Nordirlands.

Die Mournes fallen steil zur Küste der Irischen See ab. Dieses Zusammenspiel aus rauem Gebirge und glänzendem Wasser ist es, das Percy French mit den Worten „where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea" so treffend beschrieben hat.

Für Wanderbegeisterte aus Deutschland bieten die Mournes Touren für alle Schwierigkeitsgrade – von ruhigen Talwegen entlang des Silent Valley Reservoirs bis hin zu anspruchsvollen Gipfeltouren auf den Slieve Donard.

Eine bewegende Hommage

Don McLeans Interpretation von „Mountains o' Mourne" ist mehr als eine Coverversion. Es ist eine persönliche Liebeserklärung an Irland – gesungen von einem Iren in London, der in der Fremde arbeitet und von seiner Heimat träumt.

Die Melodie trägt eine melancholische Qualität in sich, die man im Englischen kaum übersetzen kann. Sie klingt nach nassem Gras, nach Nebel über den Gipfeln, nach dem Geruch von Torf – kurz: nach Irland.

McLean singt von seiner Sehnsucht nach den Mournes und von der Gewissheit, dass die Berge unverändert auf ihn warten. Der Refrain – „where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea" – zieht sich durch das gesamte Lied wie ein Anker, der den Erzähler mit seiner Heimat verbindet, egal wie weit er sich von ihr entfernt.

Ein Lied über Irland, die Menschen und die Natur

„Mountains o' Mourne“ ist auch ein Gesellschaftsporträt Percy French lässt seinen Erzähler aus dem London des späten 19. Jahrhunderts berichten – über die Baustellen, die fremden Gesichter, die merkwürdigen Sitten der Stadtbewohner. Der Humor ist zart und selbstironisch. Die Botschaft ist klar: Keine Stadt der Welt kann ersetzen, was man zu Hause zurückgelassen hat.

Für Wanderer, die die Mournes zu Fuß erkunden, wird diese Botschaft greifbar. Man spürt es in der Stille der Berge, im Wind über dem Mourne Wall, im Blick zurück auf die Küste von Carlingford Lough.

Die Liedtexte von „Mountains o' Mourne" – Original und Übersetzung

Hinweis: Die Liedtexte von Percy French / Don McLean sind urheberrechtlich geschützt. Die folgende Übersetzung dient dem Verständnis und ist keine offizielle Version.

Original (Englisch)

Übersetzung (Deutsch)

Oh, Mary, this London's a wonderful sight / With people here working by day and by night

Oh Mary, dieses London ist ein prächtiger Anblick / Hier arbeiten die Menschen bei Tag und bei Nacht

They don't sow potatoes nor barley nor wheat / But there's gangs of them diggin' for gold in the street

Sie pflanzen weder Kartoffeln noch Gerste noch Weizen / Doch Scharen von ihnen graben nach Gold in den Straßen

At least when I asked them, that's what I was told / So I just took a hand at this diggin' for gold

Zumindest sagten sie das, als ich fragte / Also versuchte ich mein Glück beim Goldgraben

But for all that I've found there, I might as well be / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Doch bei allem, was ich dort fand, könnte ich genauso gut sein / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

I believe that when writin' a wish you expressed / As to how the fine ladies of London were dressed

Ich glaube, du hast in einem Brief gefragt / Wie sich die feinen Damen Londons kleiden

But if you'll believe me, when asked to a ball / They don't wear no tops to their dresses at all

Doch wenn du mir glaubst: Wenn sie zum Ball eingeladen sind / Tragen sie gar keine Oberteile zu ihren Kleidern

Oh, I've seen them myself and you could not in truth / Tell if they were bound for a ball or a bath

Oh, ich habe sie selbst gesehen und du konntest wahrhaftig nicht sagen / Ob sie zum Ball gingen oder ins Bad

Don't be startin' them fashions now, Mary Mochree / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Fang bloß nicht mit diesen Moden an, Mary Mochree / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

There's beautiful girls here, oh, never you mind / Beautiful shapes Nature never designed

Es gibt wunderschöne Mädchen hier, oh, kümmere dich nicht darum / Schöne Formen, die die Natur nie entworfen hat

Lovely complexions of roses and cream / But let me remark with regard to the same

Liebliche Teints wie Rosen und Sahne / Doch lass mich dazu bemerken

That if at those roses you venture to sit / The colours might all come away on your lip

Dass wenn du dich diesen Rosen annäherst / Die Farben möglicherweise an deinen Lippen hängen bleiben

So I'll wait for the wild rose that's waitin' for me / In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Daher warte ich auf die wilde Rose, die auf mich wartet / An dem Ort, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

You remember young Diddy McClaren, of course / But he's over here with the rest of the force

Du erinnerst dich sicher an den jungen Diddy McClaren / Er ist hier drüben mit dem Rest der Polizei

I saw him one day as he stood on the strand / Stopped all the traffic with a wave of his hand

Ich sah ihn eines Tages, als er auf dem Strandweg stand / Mit einer Handbewegung hielt er den gesamten Verkehr an

As we were talking of days that are gone / The whole town of London stood there to look on

Während wir über vergangene Zeiten redeten / Stand ganz London da und schaute zu

But for all his great powers, he's wishful like me / To be back where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

Aber bei all seiner Macht sehnt er sich wie ich / Zurück dorthin, wo die dunkle Mourne zum Meer hinabzieht

Wandern in den Mournes – was Sie erwartet

Die Mountains of Mourne bieten einige der eindrucksvollsten Wanderwege in Nordirland. Der Mourne Wall Trail (ca. 35 km) folgt der historischen Trockensteinmauer, die im frühen 20. Jahrhundert gebaut wurde und neun Gipfel miteinander verbindet. Der Aufstieg auf Slieve Donard (ca. 14 km, 850 Hm) ist der klassische Tagestrip – anspruchsvoll, aber lohnend.

Für Wanderer, die die Mournes als Teil einer längeren Irland-Reise erkunden möchten, bietet sich unsere Cooley & Mourne Wandertour an. Sie verbindet die Halbinsel Cooley im Süden mit den Mournes im Norden – zwei Landschaften, die unterschiedlicher kaum sein könnten und doch zusammengehören wie Meer und Berg.

Praktische Informationen für Wanderer aus Deutschland

Detail

Information

Lage

County Down, Nordirland

Höchster Punkt

Slieve Donard, 849 m

Beste Reisezeit

Mai bis September

Schwierigkeitsgrad

Mittel bis anspruchsvoll

Gepäcktransport

Ja, bei geführten/selbstgeführten Touren

GPS-Navigation

Offline-App verfügbar (WHI exklusiv)

Häufig gestellte Fragen

Wie schwierig sind die Wanderwege in den Mountains of Mourne? Das hängt von der gewählten Route ab. Familienfreundliche Wege entlang von Flüssen und Stauseen sind auch für weniger geübte Wanderer geeignet. Gipfeltouren wie der Slieve Donard erfordern gute Kondition und solides Schuhwerk.

Kann man die Mournes das ganze Jahr über wandern? Ja – aber der irische Winter bringt kurze Tage, nasses Wetter und mitunter eisige Gipfel. Am besten eignen sich Mai bis September für mehrtägige Wanderungen.

Sind die Mountains of Mourne Teil eines Nationalparks? Die Mournes liegen im Mourne Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty (AONB). Ein offizieller Nationalpark ist geplant, aber noch nicht ausgerufen.

Wie komme ich als Wanderer aus Deutschland in die Mournes? Flüge nach Belfast (BFS oder BHD) sind die schnellste Verbindung. Von dort sind die Mournes mit dem Mietwagen oder Bus in etwa einer Stunde erreichbar.

Bereit für Ihre Wanderreise nach Irland?

Die Mountains of Mourne sind mehr als eine Wanderkulisse. Sie sind ein Ort, der sich ins Gedächtnis einschreibt – so wie Percy French es vor über hundert Jahren in Verse gefasst hat und Don McLean es 1971 in eine Melodie goss.

Wenn Sie die Mournes selbst erleben möchten, helfe ich Ihnen gerne dabei. Ich bin Cliff von Walking Holiday Ireland und begleite Wanderer persönlich bei der Planung ihrer Irland-Reise – von der Routenwahl bis zum letzten Übernachtungsort.

Das könnte Sie auch interessieren.

  • Die besten Wanderwege in Nordirland

  • Antrim-Küste und Causeway Coast

  • Irland – Wann ist die beste Reisezeit?

  • Hikes in Killarney National Park
content_nl:
Waar gaat 'Mountains o' Mourne' over?

"Mountains o' Mourne", gezongen door Don McLean, is een klassieke ballade die het verhaal vertelt van een man die terugdenkt aan zijn jeugdherinneringen aan de prachtige Mountains of Mourne in Ierland. Het nummer werd in 1971 voor het eerst uitgebracht door Don McLean op het album "American Pie" en is sindsdien een vaste waarde in McLeans discografie geworden.

  • Waar gaat 'Mountains o' Mourne' over?

  • Wie schreef Mountains O' Mourne?

  • Waar liggen de Mountains of Mourne?

  • Een ontroerend eerbetoon

  • Een viering van het Ierse landschap en de verbinding met menselijkheid en liefde

  • Tekst van "Mountains o' Mourne" door Don McLean

'Mountains o' Mourne'

Wie schreef Mountains O' Mourne?

Het nummer "Mountains of Mourne" werd geschreven door Percy French. Percy French werd geboren in Roscommon, Ierland en was een songwriter, componist en schilder uit de late 19e en vroege 20e eeuw. Hij stond vooral bekend om zijn humoristische en satirische liedjes en was een populaire entertainer in Ierland en Groot-Brittannië.

Wanneer schreef Percy French de ballade 'Mountains O' Mourne'?

De exacte datum waarop Percy French het nummer "Mountains of Mourne" schreef is onbekend, maar men gelooft dat het in de late 19e eeuw werd geschreven. Enkele van zijn andere bekende werken zijn: "Abdul Abulbul Amir" en "Are Ye Right, There Michael." Hij werd geboren op 1 mei 1854 en overleed op 24 juli 1920.

Waar liggen de Mountains of Mourne?

De Mountains of Mourne, gelegen in County Down, Noord-Ierland, zijn een bergketen van ruige toppen die dramatisch oprijzen uit de zee. Met hun ruige schoonheid en natuurlijke pracht hebben de Mountains veel kunstenaars en dichters geïnspireerd en zijn ze het onderwerp geweest van talloze liedjes en ballades.

Een ontroerend eerbetoon

Don McLeans uitvoering van "Mountains o' Mourne" is een eerbetoon aan het gebied en een ontroerend eerbetoon aan de kracht en schoonheid van het Ierse landschap. Het nummer wordt gekenmerkt door zijn haunting melodie en evocatieve teksten, die de luisteraar transporteren naar een wereld van mistige heuvels, glooiende groene velden en glinsterende blauwe zeeën.

McLean zingt over zijn liefde voor de Mountains of Mourne en zijn verlangen om terug te keren naar deze bijzondere plek. Het refrein van het nummer is bijzonder memorabel, met zijn herhaling van "Oh, the Mountains of Mourne, they are standing tall and free, they call to me, they call to me." Dit eenvoudige maar krachtige refrein kan sterke emoties oproepen bij luisteraars en hen herinneren aan hun herinneringen en verbindingen met de plaatsen waar ze van houden.

Een viering van het Ierse landschap en de verbinding met menselijkheid en liefde

"Mountains o' Mourne" van Don McLean is een tijdloos eerbetoon aan de schoonheid van het Ierse landschap en een viering van de menselijke verbinding met de natuurlijke wereld. Met zijn haunting melodie en evocatieve teksten blijft dit nummer het publiek boeien en zal het ongetwijfeld voor vele generaties een klassieker blijven.

https://youtu.be/I4M_0Bvk10Q

Tekst van "Mountains o' Mourne" door Don McLean

Oh, Mary, this London's a wonderful sight
With people here working by day and by night
They don't sow potatoes nor barley nor wheat
But there's gangs of them diggin' for gold in the street
At least when I asked them, that's what I was told
So I just took a hand at this diggin' for gold
But for all that I've found there, I might as well be
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

I believe that when writin' a wish you expressed
As to how the fine ladies of London were dressed
But if you'll believe me, when asked to a ball
They don't wear no tops to their dresses at all
Oh, I've seen them myself and you could not in truth
Tell if they were bound for a ball or a bath
Don't be startin' them fashions now, Mary Mochree,
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

There's beautiful girls here, oh, never you mind
Beautiful shapes Nature never designed
Lovely complexions of roses and cream
But let me remark with regard to the same
That if at those roses you venture to sit
The colours might all come away on your lip
So I'll wait for the wild rose that's waitin' for me
In the place where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea

You remember young Diddy McClaren, of course
But he's over here with the rest of the force
I saw him one day as he stood on the strand
Stopped all the traffic with a wave of his hand
As we were talking of days that are gone
The whole town of London stood there to look on
But for all his great powers, he's wishful like me
To be back where the dark Mourne sweeps down to the sea.

Klaar om je Ierse wandelavontuur te beginnen?

Laat ons de details verzorgen — accommodatie, bagagetransfers en routebeschrijvingen — zodat jij kunt genieten van elke stap van je reis door de adembenemende landschappen van Ierland.

title: Leave No Trace
content:
Leave No Trace is an Outdoor Ethics Programme designed to promote and inspire responsible outdoor recreation through education, research, and partnerships. An ethic can be defined as knowing what the right thing to do is, and doing it, even if there is no one about to see you do it.

Index leave no trace hiking

Leave No Trace depends more on attitude and awareness than on rules and regulations. Techniques designed to minimise the social and environmental impacts to the outdoors are incorporated into the Leave No Trace Outdoor Ethics Education Programme as seven principles.

The Leave No Trace programme is designed to help outdoor enthusiasts value the natural environment, to understand the impact of their activities, and to enable them to make decisions to minimise that impact while still enjoying their activities with freedom.

Plan and Prepare!

  1. Before you go check, where possible, if access is allowed and your activity is permitted in the area you wish to visit.
  2. Respect any signs, regulations, policies and special concerns for the area that you wish to visit. Permits may sometimes be needed for activities on public lands.
  3. Where possible travel by public transport or share cars; consider the availability of parking.
  4. Ensure you have the skills and equipment needed for your activity and to cope with emergencies that could arise.
  5. Check the weather forecast and always be prepared for changing weather conditions.
  6. For environmental and safety reasons, and to minimise your impact on other users, keep group numbers small; split larger parties into smaller groups.

Respect Farm Animals and Wildlife

  1. Dogs should be kept under close control and should only be brought onto hills or farmland with the landowner’s permission. Some public areas stipulate that dogs must be kept on a lead at all times, please adhere to local guidelines.
  2. Observe wild animals and birds from a distance. Avoid disturbing them, particularly at sensitive times: mating, nesting and raising young (mostly between spring and early summer).
  3. Keep wildlife wild, don’t feed wild animals or birds – our foods damage their health and leave them vulnerable to predators.
  4. Farm animals are not pets; remain at a safe distance.

Dispose of Waste Properly

  1. “If You Bring It In, Take It Out” – take home all litter and leftover food (including tea bags, fruit peels and other biodegradable foods).
  2. To dispose of solid human waste, dig a hole 15-20cms deep and at least 30m from water, campsites and tracks. Cover and disguise the hole when finished.
  3. Bring home toilet paper and hygiene products.
  4. Wash yourself or your dishes 30m away from streams or lakes and if necessary use small amounts of biodegradable soap. Bring home any solids and scatter strained dishwater.

For more information on sanitation in the outdoors read the “Where to go in the outdoors” leaflet

Minimise the Effects of Fire

  1. Fires can cause lasting impacts and be devastating to forests, natural habitats and farmland. Therefore when camping use a lightweight stove for cooking.
  2. Where fires are permitted: Use established fire rings, barbecues or create a mound fire.
  3. Keep fires small. Only use sticks from the ground that can be broken by hand. Do not use growing vegetation for use as firewood.
  4. Avoid burning plastics or other substances: which emit toxic fumes.
  5. Burn all fires to ash, put out fires completely, and then scatter cool ashes. (There are no fires allowed in the National Parks of Ireland)

Practice a Leave No Trace ethic: Make it hard for others to see or hear you and  LEAVE NO TRACE of your visit.

Be Considerate of Others

  1. Respect the people who live and work in the countryside. Park appropriately – avoid blocking gateways, forest entrances or narrow roads. Remember that farm machinery, local residents and the emergency services may need access at all times.
  2. Take care not to damage property, especially walls, fences and crops.
  3. Respect other visitors and protect the quality of their experience.
  4. Let nature’s sounds prevail. Keep noise to a minimum.

Leave What You Find

  1. Respect property. For example, farming or forestry machinery, fences, stone walls etc. Leave gates as you find them (open or closed).
  2. Preserve the past: examine – without damaging – archaeological structures, old walls and heritage artefacts, e.g. holy wells, mine workings, monuments.
  3. Conserve the present: leave rocks, flowers, plants, animals and all natural habitats as you find them. Fallen trees are a valuable wildlife habitat; do not remove or use for firewood.
  4. Avoid introducing non-native plants and animals, e.g. zebra mussels in rivers and lakes.
  5. Do not build rock cairns, structures or shelters

Travel and Camp on Durable Ground

Durable ground includes established tracks and campsites, rock, gravel, dry grasses or snow.

In popular areas:

  • Concentrate use on existing tracks and campsites.
  • To avoid further erosion, travel in single file in the middle of the track even when wet or muddy.

In more remote areas:

  • Disperse use to prevent the creation of new tracks and campsites.
  • Avoid places where impacts are just beginning to show.

If camping:

  • Protect water quality by camping at least 30m from lakes and streams.
  • Keep campsites small and discreet.

Aim to leave your campsite as you found it, or better.

title: Challenging Yet Scenic Hiking Routes In Ireland
content:
Are you an avid hiker seeking new terrains to conquer? Ireland boasts a range of hiking routes, from the most challenging to the incredibly scenic. In this blog post, we'll reveal some off-the-beaten-path trails and renowned hikes, like Benbulbin in County Sligo - one of Ireland’s toughest climbs! Join us as we explore these hidden gems and help you plan your next exciting adventure.

<

Index challenging hiking routes ireland

h3>Key Takeaways
  • Ireland offers a range of challenging and scenic hiking routes, including mountains like Benbulbin in County Sligo.
  • Some of the best challenging hikes in Ireland include The Great Sugar Loaf in County Wicklow and Carrauntoohill in County Kerry.
  • Scenic hiking routes can be found at Glenbarrow Waterfall Loop in County Laois, Ballinastoe to Djouce trail in County Wicklow, and Croaghan Cliffs in County Mayo.
  • To make the most of your hike, consider buying an Experience Ireland Guide for detailed maps and route descriptions.

Best Challenging Hiking Routes in Ireland

Discover the thrilling allure of challenging hiking routes in Ireland, from the Great Sugar Loaf in County Wicklow to Carrauntoohill in County Kerry.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hZRykzMU9YI

The Great Sugar Loaf, County Wicklow

The Great Sugar Loaf in County Wicklow is a must-see! This hike takes you up rugged quartzite slopes. It's not easy, but it's worth it. From the top of Sugarloaf Mountain, you get an amazing view.

You can see all around from this high point. The trail is about 3.3 miles long and usually takes more than two hours to finish. Many love this challenging walk in the Wicklow Mountains because it offers great sights and a good test for hikers too.

Carrauntoohill, County Kerry

Carrauntoohill is the highest mountain in Ireland and lies in County Kerry. Climbing this peak is a challenge, but it offers great views. The most liked trail here is the Devil's Ladder.

It takes you up 20 km of rough paths to reach the top. This makes Carrauntoohill a top spot for challenging hiking in Ireland.

Yet, only skilled hikers should dare to take on Carrauntoohill. You will want to be safe while enjoying your hike and exploring this tall Irish mountain range. Its height and tough trails place it among the best challenging hiking routes in Ireland.

Mweelrea, County Mayo

Mweelrea in County Mayo is not an easy walk. It has a height of 814m, making it the tallest peak in the area. The mountain's rough terrain makes for a tough hike, but also an exciting one.

If you plan to go here, bring your own map! There are no signs or paths to help guide you up the route. Despite these challenges, many climbers love Mweelrea and even take part in the Mweelrea Mountains Challenge to reach every top point.

Mt Errigal, County Donegal

Mt Errigal in County Donegal is a tall peak. It stands at a height of 751m. Hiking up this mountain is not easy. You need to make a good try and know how to get around mountains. But now, there is a gravel path that goes all the way up, which makes the climb easier.

The hike can be tough but it's worth it as you go high up on this route. It takes about 2 hours and 44 minutes for most people to finish the hike.

Lugnaquilla, County Wicklow

Lugnaquilla is a high mountain in County Wicklow, Ireland. It sits tall in the middle of the Wicklow Mountains and it's the tallest one around, except for those in Kerry County. Many people start their hike from Baravore carpark that is at the end of Glenmalure Valley.

The trail going up Lugnaquilla can be tough as there are no marked routes on it. Hikers should prepare for a good workout because this walk can range from 10 to 16 kilometers long! This means you could be hiking for about 5 to 8 hours but trust us, the view will be worth every step.

Scenic Hiking Routes in Ireland

Some of the most picturesque hiking routes in Ireland include the Glenbarrow Waterfall Loop in County Laois, the Ballinastoe to Djouce trail in County Wicklow, and the Croaghan Cliffs in County Mayo.

These routes offer stunning views of waterfalls, forests, and cliffs that will take your breath away.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-dXv6c8PCLM

Glenbarrow Waterfall Loop, County Laois

Glenbarrow Waterfall Loop is your gateway to an outdoor adventure. It lies in County Laois, in the scenic Slieve Bloom region of Ireland. You start and end at Glenbarrow trailhead for this hike.

This trail takes you along the River Barrow offering stunning riverbank views. The main attraction of this loop is the Glenbarrow Waterfall. It's a sight that makes this walk worth every step!

Ballinastoe to Djouce, County Wicklow

Ballinastoe to Djouce is a must-see trail in County Wicklow, Ireland. This long path is 17.4 km and forms a loop that starts and ends at the same spot. As you walk, you'll pass through Ballinastoe Woods, climb up White Hill and reach Djouce Mountain's peak.

This route forms part of a much bigger national hiking path that covers 128 km from Rathfarnham to Clonegal! Usually, people take about 6 hours to finish this hard but beautiful hike near Calary.

Croaghan Cliffs, County Mayo

Croaghan Cliffs in County Mayo are the highest sea cliffs in Ireland, offering stunning and scenic views. These cliffs can only be seen by hiking around or to them, making it an adventurous experience for visitors.

There is a challenging 4.9-mile out-and-back trail near Keel that takes around 4 hours and 26 minutes to complete. Hikers can expect breathtaking scenery and panoramic vistas along the way.

Croaghan Cliffs provide an opportunity for outdoor enthusiasts to enjoy the beauty of nature while tackling one of the highest points in Ireland's landscape.

Lough Ouler Loop, County Wicklow

The Lough Ouler Loop is a challenging hiking trail located in County Wicklow, Ireland. This scenic route offers breathtaking views of the surrounding area, including the beautiful Wicklow Mountains.

However, be prepared for some tough terrain as there are many boggy sections along the way and parts of the trail can be quite muddy. One of the highlights of this hike is reaching Lough Ouler, a heart-shaped lake nestled in the mountains.

It's worth noting that access to this stunning lake is only possible on foot via various trails. So if you're up for a challenge and want to experience spectacular scenery, don't miss out on exploring the Lough Ouler Loop in County Wicklow.

Bray to Greystones Cliff Walk, County Wicklow

The Bray to Greystones Cliff Walk in County Wicklow is a scenic hiking route that stretches for 7 kilometers along the Irish coastline. This walk offers breathtaking views of the ocean, green fields, and wildlife.

It is known for its stunning scenery, with dramatic cliffs and lush hillsides. Along the way, you can spot native wildlife such as Common Lizards basking in the sun. Whether you enjoy nature walks or want to take in the beauty of Ireland's landscapes, this coastal trail is a must-visit.

You'll be captivated by the picturesque sights and have plenty of opportunities for outdoor adventure in County Wicklow.

Self-Guided Walking Tours in Ireland - The Wicklow Way

The Wicklow Way is a self-guided walking tour in the east of Ireland. It is actually Ireland's oldest way-marked trail, and it covers over 127 kilometers from Dublin to Carlow. This tour offers challenging yet scenic hiking routes through various landscapes in the Wicklow region.

Along the way, travelers will be able to admire breathtaking views and explore highlights of the area. The accommodations chosen for this tour are known for their excellent location, service, and comfort.

This affordable 5-day walking holiday allows you to experience the beauty of Ireland at your own pace while enjoying the benefits of a well-planned itinerary.

https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=dgOLC28wSjU

Tips for Hiking in Ireland

Buy an Experience Ireland Guide to help you navigate the trails and find hidden gems.

Buy Experience Ireland Guide

To make the most of your hiking adventure in Ireland, consider buying the Experience Ireland Guide. This comprehensive guide will provide you with all the information you need to have a successful and enjoyable trip.

It includes detailed maps, route descriptions, and insider tips for navigating the best hiking trails in the country. Whether you're looking for family-friendly walks or tough multi-day mountain treks, this guide has got you covered.

With the Experience Ireland Guide in hand, you can explore the beautiful scenery, encounter friendly locals, and immerse yourself in Ireland's rugged landscapes. So don't miss out on this valuable resource that will enhance your hiking experience in Ireland.

Explore related stories

If you're interested in hiking in Ireland, there are plenty of related stories and resources you can explore. You can read about the best long-distance trails like the Kerry Way, known for its beautiful landscapes.

There are also articles on forest trails, loop walks, and cliff walks that showcase some of Ireland's most scenic hikes. You might want to check out popular hiking destinations like County Cork or Belfast for more trail options.

Additionally, you can join online hiking communities to connect with fellow hikers and get tips from experienced adventurers. Remember to buy an Experience Ireland Guide for more information on the best routes and trails across the country.

Join the hiking community

Connect with like-minded outdoor enthusiasts and adventure seekers by joining the vibrant hiking community in Ireland. This community provides a platform for hikers to exchange ideas, tips, and routes while exploring the stunning landscapes of this beautiful country.

Whether you're a seasoned hiker or just starting out, the hiking community offers valuable resources such as a beginner's guide to trail walking in Ireland and organized group hikes to help you make the most of your outdoor experiences.

Don't miss out on the opportunity to connect with fellow nature lovers and discover new trails together.

Conclusion

In conclusion, Ireland offers a wide range of challenging yet scenic hiking routes that cater to all levels of hikers. From the majestic peaks of County Wicklow to the rugged cliffs in Kerry and Mayo, there is something for everyone.

Whether you're a beginner looking for family-friendly walks or an experienced trekker seeking adventure, Ireland's hiking trails will not disappoint. So grab your boots, pack your bag, and get ready to explore the breathtaking landscapes that await you in beautiful Ireland.

FAQs

1. Are the hiking routes in Ireland challenging?

Yes, the hiking routes in Ireland are known for their challenging nature, with steep ascents, rugged terrains, and varying levels of difficulty.

2. Which hiking route in Ireland offers scenic views?

The Wicklow Way is a popular hiking route in Ireland that offers stunning scenic views of mountains, lakes, forests, and countryside.

3. How long does it take to complete a typical hike in Ireland?

The duration of a hike in Ireland varies depending on the chosen trail and individual pace. It can range from a few hours to multiple days for longer routes like the Kerry Way or the Dingle Peninsula Loop.

4. Do I need special gear or equipment for hiking in Ireland?

It is recommended to have appropriate gear such as sturdy footwear with good traction, waterproof clothing, sun protection, navigation tools like maps or GPS devices, and sufficient food and water for longer hikes.

5. Can beginners attempt these challenging hiking routes in Ireland?

While some hiking routes may be more suitable for experienced hikers due to their difficulty level, there are also trails available that cater to beginners or those with less experience. It's important to choose a route that matches your skill level and prepare accordingly.

title: Discover the 15 Best Places To Visit In Northern Ireland
content:
15 Best Places To Visit In Northern Ireland

Northern Ireland is an absolute gem of a country, nestled in the far corner of our beautiful Island of Ireland. Northern Ireland has everything from rolling green countryside to stunning coastal cliffs to captivating ancient castles; Northern Ireland really has it all!

Index places to visit northern ireland

This richly historical nation will provide endless exploration opportunities for anyone looking for an unforgettable journey into Irish culture and heritage. Discover why this breathtaking destination should be at the top spot on your list today!

1. The Giant's Causeway coastal route

The Giant's Causeway is a 3-billion-year-old testament to Mother Nature! This extraordinary UNESCO World Heritage Site in Northern Ireland offers tourists an unforgettable experience. Step onto the basalt columns, forged from ancient volcanic eruptions, and explore its breathtaking beauty that has been drawing visitors for centuries.

A guided tour or leisurely stroll along the picturesque coastal path will give you stunning views of this magical wonder - don't miss it on your next trip abroad!

2. The Dark Hedges

Northern Ireland is home to the beautiful Dark Hedges, located near Stranocum village in County Antrim, this remarkable site has become one of Northern Ireland's most iconic destinations and is not to be missed! Originally planted in the eighteenth century by James Stuart as an entrance to the foreboding Gracehill House, this majestic scenery has become a globally recognized feature through its place in the popular television series Game of Thrones.

Fans of the show flock to Northern Ireland for a glimpse into a fictional universe that was born from this enchanting forest hallway. Though named for something sinister and foreboding, the Dark Hedges have become beloved to many throughout Northern Ireland, spinning tales of magic and wonder while bearing testament to our collective fascination with fantasy adventures.

3. Dunluce Castle

Discover the history and hauntings of mythical Dunluce Castle in Northern Ireland. This medieval wonder perched atop a cliff overlooking the Irish Sea has stood as an iconic symbol since its construction eight centuries ago. Explore true tales of battles, sieges and apparitions with a guided tour, or stand amidst its crumbling walls to admire breathtaking views that promise unforgettable memories!

4. The Mourne Mountains

From its rolling hills to spectacular coastline, the Mourne Mountains offer some of County Down's most impressive outdoor experiences. A true paradise for adventurers, this natural beauty is perfect for hikers looking to explore winding paths and cyclists wanting a challenge on rugged terrain.

Whether it be rock climbing or simply admiring tranquility of its calm lakes - you won't want your time in The Mournes come to an end!

5. Belfast

Belfast is the colorful heart of Northern Ireland, where history and culture come alive. From top attractions like Titanic Belfast to historical gems such as Belfast Castle and Ulster Museum - there's something for everyone!

And don't forget about its lively music scene; with traditional Irish pubs and live music venues dotted throughout this vibrant city, it promises a memorable adventure.

6. The Causeway Coastal Route

Take the Causeway Coastal Route and experience one of the world's most remarkable drives! Witness stunning views of Northern Ireland as you journey between Belfast and Londonderry.

With rolling hills, historic landmarks, and a awe-inspiring coastal landscape to explore - this is an opportunity that shouldn't be missed for any traveler looking for more than just sightseeing.

7. The Old Bushmills Distillery

The Old Bushmills Distillery is a working distillery located in the village of Bushmills in County Antrim. This historic site is said to be the oldest licensed whiskey distillery in the world and offers visitors a fascinating insight into the history and heritage of Irish whiskey.

Visitors can take a guided tour of the distillery, sample some of the famous Bushmills whiskey, and learn about the traditional techniques used in its production.

8. The Gobbins and amazing cliff face Walk

Northern Ireland boasts one of the country's most unique and rewarding outdoor activities, and the Gobbins Cliff Face Walk is definitely on of the Places To Visit In Northern Ireland. Located just outside of Belfast in the spectacular Glens of Antrim, this is the perfect combination of a hill walk and hiking experience. Those who walk along The Gobbins will be treated to an array of impressive sea cliffs, tunnels, bridges and paths carved into the basalt rock that lead to stunning bird's-eye views of Northern Ireland's rugged coastline.

With its tricky suspension bridges over crashing waves, many steps and ladders as well as narrow pathways - it's no wonder The Gobbins has become one of Northern Ireland's premier adventures for both nature enthusiasts and thrill seekers alike.

9. Glens of Antrim

Northern Ireland is known for its beautiful and expansive natural scenery, and the Glens of Antrim offer some of the best hillwalking opportunities around. Located just outside Belfast on Northern Ireland’s stunning Causeway Coast, this region is perfect for both novice and experienced hikers alike.

The glens provide great views of Northern Ireland's lush valleys and low-lying hills, with plenty of options for walks along the coast or up into the highlands. With several walking trails ranging in difficulty, there is something for every level of hiker in this breathtaking part of Northern Ireland.

10. The Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge

Places To Visit In Northern Ireland must include the spectacular Causeway Coast is renowned around the world and is an essential destination for any traveller to Northern Ireland. One of the highlights of a Northern Ireland hillwalk or hiking tour is a visit to the Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge, which stretches from Northern Ireland to Carrickarede Island.

Established over 350 years ago by salmon fishermen, this daring rope bridge spans 20 meters across a chasm created by violent seas. Those brave enough to try it will be rewarded with stunning views of the Northern Coast and nearby Giant's Causeway.

With its captivating history and gorgeous backdrop, the Carrick-a-Rede Rope Bridge draws in thousands of tourists each year – definitely something that's not to be missed if you're visiting Northern Ireland!

11. The National Trust Properties

Northern Ireland is home to an extensive number of compelling and historically significant National Trust Properties. For natural beauty seekers, there are many hillwalks and hikes to explore throughout Northern Ireland.

Castle Ward House in Northern Ireland is one of the best places to visit. Owned and managed by the National Trust, Castle Ward House provides a window into the past due to its unique architecture, which appears straight out of an 18th-century movie set. It stands majestically on the shores of Strangford Lough. It includes an imposing walled garden with several walks around the estate dedicated to writing inspiration for its famed literary associations.

Inside, visitors will be delighted by an interesting digest of historical collections from military artifacts to artwork from local artisans. Best of all, you can explore this piece of history through various interactive workshops and events funded from the interpretation projects that support heritage tourism within Northern Ireland.

12. The Londonderry Walls

The Londonderry Walls are an iconic Northern Ireland attraction. Found only in Northern Ireland's second city, Derry (also known as Londonderry), the walls have been standing for over 400 years and on a sunny summer day, there's no better way to explore the city than by walking along them.

The journey will take you around the old city of Derry where you can discover hidden gems and experience first-hand what life was like centuries ago. If you're looking for a unique way to explore Northern Ireland, make sure to add a visit to Derry to your next trip; its rich history and charming atmosphere will surely leave you with unforgettable memories!

13. The National Trust Country Parks

Northern Ireland boasts some of the most stunning landscapes and breathtaking views in Europe, making it the perfect place to explore. One of the best ways to do this is with a trip to one of the National Trust Country Parks, where you can enjoy walks along cliff paths, beaches and countryside trails without a care in the world.

Whether you’re looking for stunning views, historical monuments, or just a stroll in nature, Northern Ireland's National Trust Country Parks are a great place to experience all that Northern Ireland has to offer.

With an abundance of wildlife and plenty of hiking trails marked out for visitors to make their way around, these scenic locations give people an unrivalled opportunity to get closer to Northern Ireland’s nature.

14. The North Coast Surf Schools

Northern Ireland is the perfect destination for anyone looking for a little outdoor adventure. From the beautiful coast to craggy cliffs, Northern Ireland has something for everyone. For surfers, Northern Ireland's majestic coastline provides an epic backdrop for some of the best waves in the world. Not only is Northern Ireland great for surfing, but it's also ideal for walking and all sorts of other outdoor activities.

With its breathtaking scenery, Northern Ireland offers travelers a chance to explore and experience nature in a way that they might not have before. Whether you’re just getting into surfing or are already an experienced wave-rider, Northern Ireland will definitely provide you with unforgettable memories and breath-taking views.

15. Strangford Lough

Strangford Lough, a stunning coastal lake located in Northern Ireland, is an ideal spot for outdoor enthusiasts looking to enjoy a break from the hustle and bustle of city life. Walking through its lush green landscape provides an opportunity to experience Northern Ireland's breathtaking natural beauty.

It's also home to a wide range of outdoor activities, such as kayaking, fishing, and horseriding, allowing visitors to take full advantage of the area's rich and diverse environment. Strangford Lough is the perfect destination for anyone looking for a tranquil getaway that offers stunning views and exceptional activities.

Is Northern Ireland worth seeing?

Northern Ireland is a beautiful and fascinating country, with a rich history and culture, and stunning natural beauty. Whether you're interested in exploring historic landmarks, admiring scenic landscapes, or simply relaxing on the beach, Northern Ireland has something to offer everyone. So why not pack your bags and head to this amazing country today?

Frequent questions about visiting Northern Ireland

When is the best time to visit Northern Ireland?

If you're looking for a vibrant Northern Ireland experience, summertime is the destination. From June to August, temperatures are warm and days long - perfect for tourists! But if you prefer peace and quiet or like getting great deals on flights & hotels, consider visiting in autumn or winter instead; small crowds plus lower prices make it worth braving cooler weather conditions from December-February. No matter when your adventure happens though – all four seasons have something special waiting in store.

What are some must-see attractions in Northern Ireland?

Northern Ireland is a must-visit destination, offering an array of incredible attractions. Lose yourself among the otherworldly rock formations at the Giant's Causeway or take in views from atop the Carrick-a Rede Rope Bridge for an unforgettable experience! Learn about one of history’s greatest tragedies at Titanic Belfast before checking out iconic shooting locations such as Dark Hedges - made famous by Game Of Thrones. Intrigued? The historic city Derry/Londonderry and gorgeous Mourne Mountains also await you along with breathtaking Glens Antrim – making Northern Ireland just extra special!

What is the currency in Northern Ireland, and can I use euros?

Northern Ireland is part of the United Kingdom and shares their currency: Pounds Sterling (£), which should be your go-to for smaller transactions. Although some merchants will indeed accept euros, you'll get the most bang for your buck with pounds in hand!

Do I need a visa to visit Northern Ireland?

Plan your next Northern Ireland getaway worry-free! If you’re from the European Union or the European Economic Area, no visa is required. But if not don't fret; just check out the UK government website for details on any possible visa requirements – so you can bask in all of Belfast's beauty without a hitch!

What is the best way to get around Northern Ireland?

Want to explore the mysteries of Northern Ireland? Whether you're keen on sightseeing in bustling cities like Belfast and Derry/Londonderry or exploring the countryside, there's a method of transportation best suited for your journey! Hop onboard one of their public trains or buses for an urban adventure. If it’s outdoor exploration that gets your heart racing, rent yourself a car and traverse through nature at your own pace. Or go with expert guides from various tour companies who know all the hidden gems this beautiful nation has to offer - now isn't that worth checking out?

What are some traditional Northern Irish dishes that I should try?

Northern Irish cooking has something for everyone! Savor the savory flavors of a traditional Irish stew, complete with lamb or beef and vegetables. Spice up your plate with Champ- mashed potatoes paired perfectly with scallions. Get an early start to each day the 'Ulster Fry' way - sizzling bacon, sausages, black & white pudding plus fried eggs served on potato bread - yum! And don't forget about those classic carb loaded favorites like soda bread & wheaten brea...these will be sure to keep you going all morning long; slather them in butter and jam or honey for extra tasty indulgence. Bon Appétit

What are some popular shopping destinations in Northern Ireland?

Explorers of Northern Ireland won't want to miss out on the amazing shopping opportunities! From Belfast's Victoria Square, where you can find over 70 exciting stores, cafes and restaurants - or Derry/Londonderry’s Foyleside Shopping Centre with its 50+ retail outlets. Plus Newry boasts an impressive Buttercrane Centre packed full of goodies across more than 70 shops. And don’t forget all those inviting independent boutiques scattered around this magical country – they have a unique selection of locally made products that are truly one-of-a kind!

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

title: Hiking in Donegal: Slieve League and Mount Errigal
content:
Practical Essentials: Getting Here, When to Go, Where to Stay

Getting to Donegal

By Car: The most practical option. Donegal is 3–4 hours' drive from Dublin, 2 hours from Derry. Roads are good but narrow in places; allow extra time in winter or poor visibility.

Index hiking donegal slieve league

By Bus: Bus Éireann and GoBus operate routes from Dublin and Derry to Donegal town (the main hub). Journey times are long (5+ hours from Dublin) but affordable. From Donegal town, local buses serve main villages, though connections to trailheads can be limited. Combine with local taxis for mountain access.

Where to Base Yourself:

  • **Donegal Town:** The obvious hub. Good accommodation range, restaurants, pubs, and bus connections. Slightly touristy but genuinely welcoming.
  • **Carrick:** Closer to Slieve League (ideal if that's your focus). Smaller, quieter, excellent local accommodation and restaurants.
  • **Gweedore/Dunlewy:** Central for Errigal and Glenveagh routes. More Irish-language area; authentic feel; good walking community.
  • **Glencolmcille:** Perfect base for coastal routes. Remote, beautiful, simple accommodation. Genuinely feels like the edge of Ireland.

For multi-day walking holidays, we arrange luggage transfers between accommodation, meaning you walk at your own pace without carrying a heavy pack. It transforms the experience, especially over multiple days.

Best Season

  • **Summer (June–August):** Longest days, most reliable weather (though rain is still frequent), busiest trailheads. Midges can be annoying in valleys.
  • **Autumn (September–October):** Ideal season. Weather improving, fewer crowds, spectacular colours, and clearer air. September is often the driest month.
  • **Spring (April–May):** Lengthening days, wildflowers (especially bluebells and rhododendrons), greening landscape. Weather still unpredictable.
  • **Winter (November–March):** Shortest days, harshest weather, but dramatic clouds and light. Beautiful for experienced walkers; challenging for others.

What to Pack

Donegal weather is Atlantic-facing and genuinely changeable. Pack:

  • Waterproof jacket and trousers (essential, not optional)
  • Windproof layers
  • Warm mid-layer fleece or merino
  • Waterproof pack cover
  • Good hiking boots with ankle support (scree and boggy sections demand them)
  • Spare socks and hat
  • Sunscreen and sunglasses (UV is strong at altitude, even on overcast days)
  • Waterproof bag for phone and essentials
  • Snacks and water bottle

Weather can change in minutes. The principle is "prepare for the worst, hope for the best."

Getting Around While Hiking

If you're doing a multi-day walking holiday with luggage transfers, you simply walk to your next accommodation each day. We handle packing, transportation, and logistics—you carry only a small daypack (with lunch, water, and essentials).

For day hikes, arrange transport via local taxi firms or (if you're driving) leave a car at your endpoint and arrange collection. Some walks can be done as out-and-back routes if you prefer returning to the same accommodation.

Practical Tips for Hiking in Donegal

Safety Considerations

  • **Weather:** Conditions change rapidly. Always check forecasts before heading out, but expect them to be partly wrong. Visibility can drop from excellent to near-zero within minutes.
  • **Navigation:** Trails are generally waymarked, but in poor visibility, a map and compass (or GPS) are essential. Phone signal is patchy at altitude.
  • **Bog Walking:** Much of Donegal is bog. Paths can be wet and slippery. Bog is slower than grass—allow extra time. Never try to "shortcut" across bog if the path disappears; return to your last known point and navigate carefully.
  • **Mountain Streams:** After rain, streams become torrents. Crossings that are trivial in dry weather become dangerous. Don't attempt to cross fast water.

Making the Most of Your Walking Holiday

  • **Start with One Man's Pass:** If you do Slieve League, the ridge is unforgettable. It's exposed but safe; the scramble is manageable.
  • **Climb Errigal Early:** It's popular, so ascending before 10 am means summit views without crowds.
  • **Discover Glenveagh Slowly:** Don't rush through the park. Walk the easier Doon Level Trail one day, Spinetail the next. The park reveals itself gradually.
  • **Talk to Locals:** Donegal walkers are genuinely helpful. They'll give you honest advice about conditions, hidden routes, and excellent places to eat.

Ready to Explore Donegal?

Hiking in Donegal is about more than collecting peaks. It's about standing on sea cliffs watching Atlantic storms approach, climbing mountains that feel genuinely wild, and discovering valleys where sheep outnumber people. It's the kind of walking holiday that reminds you why you fell in love with mountains in the first place.

If you're planning a Donegal walking holiday and want to walk at your own pace without worrying about logistics, we offer self-guided routes across the region with full support—accommodation arranged in handpicked B&Bs, luggage transfers handled, and detailed route notes provided. You carry only essentials and focus on enjoying every step.

Whether this is your first visit to Donegal or you're returning to explore new routes, these trails are calling. The question isn't whether you can do them—it's when you'll start planning your trip. Let's get you to Donegal's peaks and cliffs.

Ready to start planning your Donegal walking holiday? Browse our Donegal walking tours and discover why these mountains matter so deeply to Irish walkers.

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?

Let us take care of the details — accommodation, luggage transfers, and route notes — so you can focus on enjoying every step of your journey through Ireland's stunning landscapes.

Walk the Wicklow Way

The Hidden Gems: Beyond the Classics

Glenveagh National Park Routes

Glenveagh National Park deserves its own article (and we've written one if you want deep detail), but it's essential to mention here because it offers something different from Slieve League and Errigal. Instead of exposed peaks, Glenveagh delivers deep, green valleys, clear mountain rivers, and genuine solitude.

The Doon Level Trail (3 km, 1.5 hours, easy):

This is Glenveagh at its most accessible. A waymarked path follows an old estate road through native woodland, alongside the pristine Doon River. It's flat, scenic, and perfect for walkers seeking something gentler or families with children. The river views are peaceful; the woodland feels genuinely secluded. In summer, the glen is emerald green; in autumn, the colours are extraordinary.

The Spinetail Trail (6 km, 3–4 hours, moderate):

For something more substantial, this circular route climbs from glen floor to open moorland, offering panoramic views across the park's mountains and down to Lough Veagh. The upper sections are exposed; weather and visibility matter here. But on clear days, the 360-degree views make it feel like you're on top of the world. Walkers describe it as the route that best captures Glenveagh's scale and grandeur.

Why Glenveagh Matters: It's where Irish wilderness feels intimate rather than intimidating. Getting to Donegal

By Car: The most practical option. Donegal is 3–4 hours' drive from Dublin, 2 hours from Derry. Roads are good but narrow in places; allow extra time in winter or poor visibility.

By Bus: Bus Éireann and GoBus operate routes from Dublin and Derry to Donegal town (the main hub). Journey times are long (5+ hours from Dublin) but affordable. From Donegal town, local buses serve main villages, though connections to trailheads can be limited. Combine with local taxis for mountain access.

Where to Base Yourself:

  • **Donegal Town:** The obvious hub. Good accommodation range, restaurants, pubs, and bus connections. Slightly touristy but genuinely welcoming.
  • **Carrick:** Closer to Slieve League (ideal if that's your focus). Smaller, quieter, excellent local accommodation and restaurants.
  • **Gweedore/Dunlewy:** Central for Errigal and Glenveagh routes. More Irish-language area; authentic feel; good walking community.

Best Season

  • **Summer (June–August):** Longest days, most reliable weather (though rain is still frequent), busiest trailheads. Midges can be annoying in valleys.

What to Pack

Donegal weather is Atlantic-facing and genuinely changeable. Pack:

  • Waterproof jacket and trousers (essential, not optional)

Practical Tips for Hiking in Donegal

Safety Considerations

  • **Weather:** Conditions change rapidly. Always check forecasts before heading out, but expect them to be partly wrong. Visibility can drop from excellent to near-zero within minutes.

  • **Mountain Streams:** After rain, streams become torrents. Crossings that are trivial in dry weather become dangerous. Don't attempt to cross fast water.

  • **Climb Errigal Early:** It's popular, so ascending before 10 am means summit views without crowds.

The Classic Routes: What Makes Each Special

Slieve League: The Dramatic Coastal Climb

Slieve League (Sliabh Liag) isn't just Donegal's highest sea cliff—it's one of Ireland's most cinematogenic walking routes, and the reputation is completely justified. The cliffs rise 601 metres straight from the Atlantic, making them nearly twice the height of their famous Cliffs of Moher cousins. But it's not just about the vertical drama; it's the sense of exposure, the howling Atlantic wind, and those moments when you round a ridge and the landscape simply stops, dropping away into blue.

The Classic Slieve League Ridge Walk (8–9 km, 4–5 hours, moderate to strenuous):

This is the walker's choice. Rather than tackling it from below (a steep, boggy slog), most experienced hikers start from the Cliff Edge carpark and traverse the dramatic ridge, descending toward Carrigan Head. You'll encounter One Man's Pass—a narrow, exposed section that looks genuinely intimidating but is perfectly manageable with care and common sense. The scrambling is minimal; the exposure is maximum. Walkers with a head for heights will find it exhilarating; those prone to vertigo might prefer approaching from below.

The coastal views are astonishing: you're looking down at storm-carved caves, out toward Tory Island and the Aran Islands, and back across Donegal Bay. The ridge walk captures what makes Donegal special—genuine wildness, minimal infrastructure, and the feeling that you're walking somewhere few people venture.

Slieve League Loop (10 km, 5–6 hours, strenuous):

If you want the full experience without the scramble, the longer loop combines the ridge traverse with gentler moorland walking. You'll climb from Carrigan Head via open bog, cross high plateaus with 360-degree views, and descend back toward the coast. It's tougher underfoot (bog sections can be wet and slow) but equally rewarding.

Getting to Slieve League: Head to Carrick (a small village in southwest Donegal) and follow signs toward Teelin. The Cliff Edge carpark is the trailhead. Allow 1.5 hours' drive from Donegal town. The road narrows significantly as you approach; in peak season, carpark capacity is limited. Going early is wise.

Mount Errigal: The Signature Cone

Mount Errigal (Portuguese: An Earagail) is Donegal's most iconic mountain—that distinctive conical peak you've probably seen in photographs, sitting alone in the northeast of the county. At 752 metres, it's not the highest in Donegal, but its isolated position and perfect cone shape make it feel grander than its altitude suggests.

The Direct Ascent (5 km, 2.5–3.5 hours, moderate):

From the Dunlewy carpark, a well-trodden but steep path climbs the northeast flank, gaining 700 metres of elevation. The lower slopes are grassy, but the upper mountain is scree and loose rock—watch your footing, especially on descent. The reward is a summit with views stretching across Donegal's interior mountains, westward to the Atlantic, and on clear days across to the Scottish coast.

The mountain attracts weekend crowds (especially in summer), but it's busy rather than overcrowded. Start early if you prefer solitude. The scree descent is fun and quick; many walkers actually enjoy the descent more than the climb.

Why Errigal Matters: It's approachable enough for confident intermediate walkers, dramatic enough to feel genuinely mountainous, and iconic enough that summiting it becomes a true memory marker. If you're spending a walking holiday in Donegal, Errigal absolutely deserves a day.

Getting to Errigal: Head toward Gweedore (An Gaoth Dobhair) in central Donegal. The Dunlewy carpark is well signposted. About 45 minutes from Donegal town.

The Hidden Gems: Beyond the Classics

Glenveagh National Park Routes

Glenveagh National Park deserves its own article (and we've written one if you want deep detail), but it's essential to mention here because it offers something different from Slieve League and Errigal. Instead of exposed peaks, Glenveagh delivers deep, green valleys, clear mountain rivers, and genuine solitude.

The Doon Level Trail (3 km, 1.5 hours, easy):

This is Glenveagh at its most accessible. A waymarked path follows an old estate road through native woodland, alongside the pristine Doon River. It's flat, scenic, and perfect for walkers seeking something gentler or families with children. The river views are peaceful; the woodland feels genuinely secluded. In summer, the glen is emerald green; in autumn, the colours are extraordinary.

The Spinetail Trail (6 km, 3–4 hours, moderate):

For something more substantial, this circular route climbs from glen floor to open moorland, offering panoramic views across the park's mountains and down to Lough Veagh. The upper sections are exposed; weather and visibility matter here. But on clear days, the 360-degree views make it feel like you're on top of the world. Walkers describe it as the route that best captures Glenveagh's scale and grandeur.

Why Glenveagh Matters: It's where Irish wilderness feels intimate rather than intimidating. The park has excellent infrastructure (visitor centres, facilities), but the wild landscape feels completely preserved. If you're doing a multi-day Donegal walking holiday, Glenveagh should anchor one or two days.

Summer temperatures range from 14–18°C; winter drops to 4–8°C. The wind chill is real. But this is also why Donegal feels so alive—you're walking in genuinely wild landscape, not a park-managed experience.

Whether this is your first visit to Donegal or you're returning to explore new routes, these trails are calling. The question isn't whether you can do them—it's when you'll start planning your trip. Let's get you to Donegal's peaks and cliffs.

title: How To Choose A Historically Rich Hiking Destination In Ireland
content:
Choosing a historically rich hiking destination in Ireland can feel overwhelming with so many options to choose from. After all, the Emerald Isle is littered with centuries-old landmarks and captivating sceneries every hiker would dream of.

<

Index historical hiking destination ireland

p>Luckily, this blog post will guide you through some top picks and factors to consider when selecting your trail, making the process less daunting. So let's dive into discovering tyre tracks of Ireland’s remarkable past on foot!

Key Takeaways

  • Ireland offers a plethora of historically rich hiking destinations, such as the Hill of Tara Walk, Ballyteige Burrow, Queen Maeve Trail, Diamond Hill in Connemara National Park, and Glendalough and the Spinc Cliffs.
  • Factors to consider when choosing a historical hiking destination include accessibility, level of difficulty, historical significance, and scenic beauty.
  • The Wicklow Way is a famous self - guided walking tour in Ireland that covers 127 kilometers from Dublin to Carlow and provides beautiful views along the way.
  • Late summer to autumn is considered the best time to travel for historical hiking in Ireland. It's important to pack necessary equipment like raincoats, insulating clothing, durable hiking shoes, headgear for protection against rain and sun exposure.

Historical Hiking Destinations in Ireland

Some historically rich hiking destinations in Ireland include the Hill of Tara Walk, Ballyteige Burrow, Queen Maeve Trail, Diamond Hill in Connemara National Park, and Glendalough and the Spinc Cliffs.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zPNuXnt32p4

Hill of Tara Walk

The Hill of Tara Walk is a great place in Ireland. The hill is near Skryne, County Meath. Once, it was used for big events and as a burial site during the Iron Age. It's seen as one of Europe’s top old sites.

There are many trails here you can walk or hike on. This spot is perfect for people who love Irish history and culture.

Ballyteige Burrow

Ballyteige Burrow is a must-see for hikers. This 9km-long coastal area is full of special spots to discover. It's known worldwide because it has so many different features. You will spot the rare lichen species, Fulgensia fulgens, here.

It's not just about nature though. Ballyteige Burrow makes you feel like you've gone back in time! You'll see true pieces of Ireland's past all around you. There are old ruins and monuments that have been fixed up for you to enjoy.

Plus, the Kilmore Quay - Ballyteige Burrow Trail is rated as a top hike at this site.

Queen Maeve Trail

The Queen Maeve Trail is a sight to see in County Sligo, Ireland. It's high up on Knocknarea and has more than 500 wooden steps. They guide you right up to the top of the hill. There you'll find a big old cairn called Miosgan Meadhbha.

People link this spot with Queen Maeve from long ago times. The site matters for both its rich history and ritual uses of old. From here, there are great views that let eyes drink in all of Sligo and Ballisodare Bay down below by the coast.

You will feel like you've stepped into an ancient tale just being there!

Diamond Hill in Connemara National Park

Diamond Hill stands tall in Connemara National Park, County Galway. It reaches up to 445 meters and gives stunning views of the land around it. The trip up is a bit tough but worth it for the sights you can see from the top.

Many people who love nature and outdoor fun choose this place to hike.

Glendalough and the Spinc Cliffs

Glendalough is a spot in Ireland full of beauty and old tales. St. Kevin founded it as an Early Medieval monastic settlement. It is also known for the Spinc Cliffs that look over Glendalough Valley.

Many hikers love this ridge. There's a great walk called the Spinc and Glenealo Valley Walk, which takes about 3.5 hours to finish. The height climb during this 9km walk is up to 410m high! At its highest point, you'll reach a stunning 500m above the valley floor.

Self-Guided Walking Tours: The Wicklow Way

The Wicklow Way is a famous path in Ireland. It was the first trail with way marks in the country. The trail starts in Dublin and ends in Carlow, covering 127 kilometers. As you walk, you will see green lands that look like emeralds.

This makes for great views all through your trip.

On this tour, there are places to sleep after each day's hike. These homely stops are chosen by hands and run by families. Most are B&Bs or small hotels, so they feel friendly and cosy.

You also get breakfast every morning of the tour!

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AqLAz2GXPQI

Factors to Consider When Choosing a Historical Hiking Destination

Consider the accessibility, level of difficulty, historical significance, and scenic beauty when choosing a historical hiking destination.

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xTy8kroNnog

Accessibility

Hiking in Ireland can be fun for all. Many trails are open to everyone, even those with mobility issues. Places like Glenveagh National Park have paths that are easy to move on. They take good care of their trails too.

This makes it a great place for nature walks if you're on a wheelchair. With the right search terms, you can find more places like this near Dublin and other parts of Ireland. It's good to know that these outdoor activities are not just for some people but suit seniors and disabled persons as well.

Level of Difficulty

Hiking trails in Ireland come with clear difficulty level markings, ranging from easy to hard. This means that you can easily choose a trail that matches your hiking abilities and preferences.

If you're up for a challenge, there are some hiking destinations in Ireland that can test your skills. On the other hand, if you prefer a more relaxed hike, there are plenty of shorter and easier family-friendly walks to enjoy.

Whether you're looking for a tough multi-day mountain trek or a leisurely stroll through historical sites, Ireland offers a wide range of hiking options for all experience levels. So no matter what your skill level is, you'll find the perfect trail to explore and discover the natural beauty and rich history of Ireland's historical hiking destinations.

Historical Significance

Ireland is filled with hiking destinations that hold great historical significance. From ancient civilizations to archaeological sites, these trails offer a chance to explore the rich cultural heritage of the country.

Glenveagh National Park, for example, provides a combination of scenic beauty and historical ruins that showcase Ireland's dramatic history. Whether you're interested in heritage trails or simply love exploring historical landmarks, Ireland has plenty to offer for those seeking a meaningful and educational hiking experience.

Scenic Beauty

Ireland is known for its stunning scenic beauty, with coastal landscapes, mountainous terrain, and breathtaking views of the Atlantic Ocean. One of the most spectacular places to experience this beauty is on the cliffs of Sliabh Liag, where you can stand on the edge of Europe and gaze out at the endless ocean.

Whether it's exploring Glendalough and its picturesque lakes or hiking through Connemara National Park and admiring its natural wonders, Ireland offers a wealth of scenic hiking destinations that will leave you in awe.

Soak in the stunning surroundings as you walk along historic trails and immerse yourself in Ireland's rich cultural heritage.

Planning Your Historical Hiking Trip in Ireland

When planning your historical hiking trip in Ireland, consider the best time to travel, necessary equipment, and transportation options in the country.

Best Time to Travel

Late summer to autumn, particularly the months of August to October, is considered the prime time for historical hiking in Ireland. During this period, you can experience challenging landscapes and secluded paths that offer a more adventurous experience.

It's also a popular time for international visitors to Ireland, so considering crowd levels when planning your trip is important. In addition, October in Ireland showcases beautiful autumnal colors and refreshing weather.

If you're looking for remote trails and challenging terrain, late summer to autumn is the optimal period for hiking in Ireland.

Necessary Equipment

To ensure a successful historical hiking trip in Ireland, it is important to have the necessary equipment. Here are some essential items you should pack:

  1. Raincoat: With Ireland's unpredictable weather, a waterproof jacket is a must-have to stay dry during your hike.
  2. Insulating clothing: Layers are key to keeping warm in the cool Irish climate. Pack thermal tops and bottoms, as well as fleece or wool sweaters.
  3. Durable hiking shoes: Sturdy walking or hiking boots with good grip will provide support and help prevent slips on the often uneven terrain.
  4. Headgear: Don't forget a hat or cap to protect your head from both rain and sun exposure.
  5. Sun protection lotion: Even on cloudy days, the UV rays can still harm your skin. Apply sunscreen with a high SPF before heading out.
  6. Appropriate gear: Consider packing additional items such as a windbreaker for extra protection against blustery conditions, sunglasses to shield your eyes from glare, and gloves to keep your hands warm.

Transportation options in Ireland

Ireland has a good transportation network with options like trains and buses. Buses are popular for reaching remote destinations that might not have train services. However, it's important to note that the bus service in Ireland may be infrequent, so planning ahead is recommended.

When visiting Ireland, it's a good idea to organize your transportation for the entire country to make sure you can reach all the historical hiking destinations you want to explore.

Additionally, there are luxury walking tours available in Ireland for those who prefer an organized and high-end hiking experience.

Conclusion

In conclusion, choosing a historically rich hiking destination in Ireland can be an exciting and rewarding experience. By considering factors such as accessibility, level of difficulty, historical significance, and scenic beauty, you can find the perfect hiking trail that allows you to immerse yourself in Ireland's fascinating history while enjoying its breathtaking landscapes.

So lace up your boots and get ready to uncover Ireland's historical treasures on foot!

FAQs

1. What are some historically rich hiking destinations in Ireland?

Some historically rich hiking destinations in Ireland include the Giant's Causeway, Skellig Michael, Cliffs of Moher, Hill of Tara, and Glendalough.

2. How do I choose a historically rich hiking destination in Ireland?

To choose a historically rich hiking destination in Ireland, consider your interests and preferences for historical sites, such as ancient ruins, castles, or religious landmarks. Research various destinations and their historical significance before making a decision.

3. Are there guided tours available for historically rich hiking destinations in Ireland?

Yes, there are guided tours available for many historically rich hiking destinations in Ireland. These tours can provide valuable insights into the history and cultural significance of the sites while ensuring safety during the hike.

4. Can I visit these historically rich hiking destinations without prior knowledge of Irish history?

Yes, you can still visit these historically rich hiking destinations without prior knowledge of Irish history. The sites often have informative signage or audio guides that provide relevant information about their historical background.

5. Is it safe to hike at these historically-rich locations in Ireland?

Yes! However visitors should always take precautions when exploring any natural areas like wearing appropriate footwear for difficult terrain conditions and packing adequate supplies including water bottle etc..

Ready to Start Your Irish Walking Adventure?
